title: Forcepoint ZTNA Private Application Access Latency (`forcepoint:one`)
dataSources: Forcepoint cloud SSE (`forcepoint:one`), on-prem Web Security (`websense:cg:kv`), Email (`forcepoint:email:kv`), DLP (`forcepoint:dlp`)
app: Forcepoint Insights SIEM App (Splunkbase 8053); Forcepoint Web Security TA (`websense:cg:kv`); Email Security KV (`forcepoint:email:kv`)
spl:
index=sase sourcetype="forcepoint:one" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(event_type),"(?i)ztna|private app")
| stats perc95(latency_ms) as p95_ms by app_name, connector_name
| where p95_ms>300
| sort -p95_ms
description: High latency on private apps drives users to bypass ZTNA with split tunnel tricks.
value: IT operations and ZTNA administrators monitor private application access latency through SSE connectors, identify degraded applications before users complain, and optimize connector placement by correlating latency with user location.
implementation: Install the Insights SIEM app, configure HEC inputs per product line, and align indexes (`proxy`, `email`, `dlp`, `sase`). Normalize usernames to enterprise SAMAccountName.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.Email
  by Email.user, Email.action, Email.src, Email.dest span=1h
| sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Forcepoint Web Security

knownFalsePositives: Spikes from new SaaS rollouts, automated workflows, sanctioned bulk uploads, or M&A integration.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Forcepoint Insights SIEM App (Splunkbase 8053) or direct integration from Forcepoint ONE SSE.
• Data in `index=sase` with `sourcetype=forcepoint:one`. Key fields: `event_type` (ztna/private_access), `user`, `app_name`, `app_connector`, `latency_ms`, `action` (allow/deny), `src_ip`, `src_country`, `device_posture_status`.
• Forcepoint ONE SSE with ZTNA module enabled and private application connectors deployed on-premises or in cloud environments.
• ZTNA connectors act as brokers — user traffic goes through the SSE cloud to the connector, which reaches the private application. Latency = user-to-SSE + SSE-to-connector + connector-to-app.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify ZTNA events:
```spl
index=sase sourcetype="forcepoint:one" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(event_type), "ztna|private")
| stats count avg(latency_ms) as avg_latency by app_name, action
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — ZTNA latency analysis:
```spl
index=sase sourcetype="forcepoint:one" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(event_type), "ztna|private") AND action="allow" AND isnotnull(latency_ms)
| stats count as sessions avg(latency_ms) as avg_latency p50(latency_ms) as median_latency p95(latency_ms) as p95_latency max(latency_ms) as peak_latency by app_name, app_connector
| eval status=case(p95_latency>2000, "DEGRADED", p95_latency>500, "ELEVATED", 1==1, "HEALTHY")
| sort - p95_latency
```

Understanding this SPL: Measures per-application latency through the ZTNA connector. P95 (95th percentile) is the key metric — it represents the experience of the slowest 5% of connections, which often correspond to users in remote locations or during peak load. A p95 above 2 seconds for business-critical applications like ERP or VDI degrades productivity significantly. The `app_connector` field helps isolate whether the issue is in the SSE cloud path or the connector-to-application leg.

Latency by user location:
```spl
index=sase sourcetype="forcepoint:one" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(event_type), "ztna|private") AND action="allow"
| stats avg(latency_ms) as avg_latency count by app_name, src_country
| sort app_name, - avg_latency
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Forcepoint ONE portal: ZTNA > Application Insights. Compare latency metrics with Splunk.
(b) Access a private application from different network locations and verify latency values in Splunk match the perceived experience.
(c) Check connector health: verify the `app_connector` field shows the expected connector for each application.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Forcepoint — ZTNA Performance"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: "Avg latency (ms)", "Degraded applications", "ZTNA sessions (24h)".
• Row 2 — Timechart: p95 latency by application over 7 days.
• Row 3 — Table: application, connector, avg/median/p95 latency, status.

Alert: Trigger when p95 latency exceeds 2000ms for any application over a 1h window.

Runbook:
1. **High latency for specific connector** — The connector's network path to the SSE cloud or to the application is degraded. Check connector host resources (CPU, memory, network).
2. **High latency from specific country** — Users in that region may need a closer SSE PoP. Check Forcepoint ONE's PoP location coverage.
3. **Latency spike during peak hours** — Connector capacity may be insufficient. Deploy additional connectors for load distribution.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **`latency_ms` always null** — Latency measurement may not be enabled in the ZTNA log configuration. Check event fields.
• **Latency extremely high (>10s)** — Connection may be timing out rather than completing. Check if the application is actually reachable from the connector.
• **No ZTNA events** — ZTNA module may not be enabled or no private applications are configured.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how quickly our employees can reach internal work applications through our secure access system, making sure the connection is fast enough that it does not slow them down.
